text: Positive training adaptation (improved performance) is associated with small increases in resting vagal HRV indices (RMSSD SMD=0.58, HFP SMD=0.55) in endurance-trained athletes.
Positive training adaptation produces moderate increases in post-exercise HRV (RMSSD SMD=0.60, HFP SMD=0.90, SD1 SMD=1.20) and post-exercise heart rate recovery (SMD=0.63).
specific_value: post-exercise RMSSD SMD=0.60, HFP SMD=0.90, SD1 SMD=1.20, HRR SMD=0.63

text: Overreaching (performance decline) does NOT meaningfully change resting HFP (SMD=0.04) or SD1 (SMD=0.04), making resting HRV largely unaffected by overreaching.
Post-exercise HRV and heart rate recovery increase during both positive adaptation AND overreaching, meaning these metrics alone cannot distinguish beneficial training from overreaching.
specific_value: overreaching post-exercise RMSSD SMD=0.64, HRR SMD=0.46

text: Heart rate acceleration decreases in response to overreaching (SMD=-0.48) but increases with positive adaptation (SMD=1.34), making it a potential distinguishing marker of training-induced fatigue.

Resting HRV alone is insufficient to detect overreaching; additional measures such as heart rate acceleration or performance testing are needed to determine whether training changes reflect positive or negative adaptation.
Vagal-related HRV indices (RMSSD, HFP, SD1) are the most appropriate HRV metrics for monitoring autonomic training status in endurance athletes.
